Consumer complaint narrative:
Friday, XX/XX/XXXX through XXXX, via their " excellent match '' for potential loans, I applied at Upstart.com for a personal loan of {$3500.00}. Upstart immediately denied me. During the application process, the deceptive advertising was touting this easy process that is not heavily based on credit history. Supposedly they pretend that they help people with problem credit. On paper, my credit report and FICO score is good, in the mid 700s, but for some mystery reason I can not get the type of credit I should with my scores, and it has been that way my whole life, no matter what my situation. 

I already had my credit reports, my FICO score, my credit rating way before dealing with Upstart. So they sent me a generic reason as to why I was denied. In several back and forth communications, I tried to get them to tell me the specific, exact reason why they denied me. They kept telling me everything but. 

They told me to go check my credit report if I had issues. I told them I already have my reports and I don't see any of the generic reasons they listed. 

I asked again and again to clarify why they denied me, but they would give me the credit rules and regulations, tell me why it " could be '' but never specifically tell me the reason for the denial. I even cited the law, which states the must disclose the reason for the denial, not tell people what it could be or what the law says. But at that point they just stopped responding. 

So because of tat I have a hard inquiry from Upstart and nothing to show for it. I do not think our regulations allow creditors to deny loans just because they want to, and whatever the reason they are supposed to be transparent about it so we can address it if possible. 

There is harm to my credit file now, as each inquiry brings down your score. I want to mention here that one of their " reasons '' was too many inquiries. The only inquiry I had was from a job, The other one was THEIRS. Obviously they are just making any excuse because they have a certain set of people who they will service and other people are being redlined. Otherwise why so opposed to giving their specific reason? 

Also, one of their denial responses says to check my Upstart mailbox - but I never had one. I never had to log into their website. I can not log in and it doesn't show a way to register either. So even their " link '' doesn't lead you to any information. They utilize XXXX XXXX XXXX for Georgia, so evidently they are in on this too.

Sub-product: Home equity loan or line of credit (HELOC)
Consumer complaint narrative: re : Bank of America, N.A. HELOC Initial Disclosure XXXX.BOA XX/XX/XXXX The Early Closure Fee is explained under " Fees and Charges ''. To paraphrase : BOA will pay our closing costs but if we close the HELOC prior to 36 months, BOA will charge us up to {$450.00} ( the " base fee '' ) plus any mortgage and government taxes, recording fees, and any closing agent or attorney fees BOA paid on our behalf to open the HELOC. In this section, it also states : " If you ask, we will give you an itemization of the fees you will have to pay to third parties. '' Our HELOC was approved XX/XX/XXXX. I asked for an itemization of the fees subject to recapture in the event we closed the HELOC prior to 36 months. I was told that we would receive an itemization of the fees subject to recapture on the invoice if and when we closed the line prior to 36 months, but not before closing. Applicable regulations only require BOA to disclose a cost range for third party fees prior to closing, but we argue that BOA set a higher ( and more transparent ) standard in its HELOC Initial Disclosure when it offered to provide an itemization of the 3rd party fees subject to recapture. Unfortunately, BOA failed to live up to the high standard it set forth in its own disclosure. Providing an itemization of the fees subject to recapture prior to closing would benefit both the borrower and the bank, even if not mandated by regulation. Borrowers would get to consider the cost of the third party fees before deciding to accept the loan. The bank would make the Early Closure Fee more transparent and generate fewer consumer complaints.

Consumer complaint narrative:
I am a XXXX resident using PennyMac, based in XXXX as a lender to service a mortgage. I chose PennyMac before I left XXXX to move to XXXX, because of the company 's relationship with XXXX, with whom I had prior good mortgage referrals. I very much regret having chosen this company, which has caused several delays in closing, and which nearly cost me a thousand dollars in lost seller 's credit, until my agent gave them instructions on how to do their job. However, these annoyances are not my legal complaint.
On the week before we reserved a date for our house closing, assured by PennyMac ( " PM '' ) that we could close on or beforeXX/XX/XXXX, I asked what specific power of attorney would be needed for my co-signer, my mother who lives out-of-state, to have me sign for her. My agent 's manager, who was covering for the agent who was out for a few days ( without telling me ), checked for a PM power of attorney template. Not finding one, she referred me to my title insurance company. I did this, and immediately forwarded a copy to PM to review and approve. On XX/XX/XXXX, the manager replied that she had forwarded the POA to her underwriter to do so. In the meantime, I sent a copy to my mother who paid to have it notarized and sent next-day to the title company for closing on XX/XX/XXXX The signed copy faxed to title and PM. On XX/XX/XXXX, as my mother was sending the notarized copy, PM contacted me to tell me that the underwriter refused to review the unsigned copy that they had received. After they received it, they would still require 24 hours before they would approve it.
Twenty four hours later, they declined to approve the POA. OnXX/XX/XXXX, they wrote XX/XX/XXXX POA must be fully executed prior to approval. Per Policy Procedure doc XXXX ( XXXX ) : # 1 Please provide a handwritten letter of explanation regardin the reason for the used of the Power of Attorney and relationship between parties.
# 2 The borrower ( s ) must indicate who will physically attend the signing.
My complaint against PM is that this policy is unreasonable on its face, and grossly unreasonable as applied in my particular case.
There is no rational reason for providing a " handwritten letter '' as opposed to any other form of communication, including a signed typed letter. The policy does not specify who is to write the " handwritten letter '' -- the individual seeking the POA, the other Buyer, or some other individual.
When the party seeking the POA lives out-of-state, hundreds of miles away, that is a self-evident reason for seeking a POA.
When the party seeking the POA is already documented in the company 's records as being the mother of the other Buyer, the information demanded is superfluous and should not be a condition of approval.
The POA reviewed designates the other Buyer as the one authorized to sign. Asking who will physically attend the signing serves no rational purpose and is implied on the face of the POA.
Finally, these " surprise '' requirements, as trivial and unexpected as they are, were never shared with me after the POA was discussed with PM representatives and manager, until XX/XX/XXXX. Even had we provided what we believed the underwriter needed ( which as I note is ambiguous ), we were informed that the underwriter needs 24 hours to consider any document before giving approval. Thus, our promised closing date of XX/XX/XXXXhad to be rescheduled again.
I immediately informed PM that we would withdraw the POA, as it had cost us unacceptable delays in closing, and we could not afford further delays while we worked with the underwriter to figure out what exactly they needed to approve the document. We are trying to make moving arrangements, school transfer arrangements, work arrangements, utility transfer and property maintenance arrangements. The money my mother spent on notarizing and sending the document to the title company was wasted, and she was compelled to spend several hundred dollars to fly out to be physically present at the signing.
Please investigate the PM policy, including procedures how it is shared with the public, to determine if it puts an unreasonable burden on the consumer. Consumers at the late stage of the process of closing on a house are in a highly vulnerable position, and the mortgage company has a virtual monopoly over their customer at that point. I believe that PM is negligent in its application of its policies, which are themselves arbitrary and capricious.

I questioned if that would actually be saving anything and she stated including closing costs, it would save {$2300.00} long term and THEY WOULD REMOVE THE PMI MORTGAGE INSURANCE WITH OUT NEEDING ANY APPRAISAL. Consumer complaint narrative:
I have XXXX debit card transactions that occurred a day prior to an ATM withdrawal ( ATM withdrawal resulted in an overdraft of the account ). However, the ATM withdrawal ( which occurred a day after the debit transactions ) posted to the account before the XXXX debit card transactions, so instead of an overdraft fee ( {$35.00} ) for the ATM withdrawal I received XXXX overdraft fees ( {$140.00} ). XXXX for the withdrawal and XXXX for each of the debit transactions. I called the bank ( Wells Fargo ) and spoke with a customer service agent and then a supervisor ( XXXX XXXX ). The supervisor repeatedly refused to provide contact info for his manager, or anyone above him, and repeatedly stated " We are all going to tell you the same thing ''. He eventually transferred me to a department so I could " file a complaint '', but when transferred I was asked to enter an extension from the directory and the line disconnected. I was given no such information from the supervisor who transferred me. 

I have researched the practice of batching the posts for the day and then posting the highest items first. I understand this is a " legal '' practice. However, the overdraft item ( ATM withdrawal ) occurred a day after the debit card transactions. So in my case, this should not apply. 

Which raises another concern. The supervisor I spoke with notified me that all three of the transactions were listed as " Credit '' and not " Debit ''. XXXX of these transactions are from a market right across the street from my employer. I visit this place almost daily and I ALWAYS select " Debit '' and enter my PIN. There is absolutely no chance that I accidentally ran it as credit. 

So the second component of my complaint is that assured " Debit '' transactions are showing up as " Credit '' transactions once processed. I have been told by multiple agents ( Wells Fargo customer service ) over the years that as long as I run the transaction as " Debit '' the funds will come out of my account immediately ( If ran as credit there is a valid delay in posting, depending on the merchant ). I have an inclination that the supervisor I spoke with may have been misleading about my transactions posting as " Credit '' in order to give a reason why the fees were valid, but I have no evidence or documentation to confirm this. 

To summarize, my bank has issued XXXX overdraft fees ( {$35.00} each ) based on the posting order of items. However, XXXX of the transactions were made when there were sufficient funds in the account. And further, the transaction that caused the overdraft occurred a day after the other transactions. I know my fees are relatively low for filing a formal complaint, but I tried contacting the bank for assistance and feel I have nowhere else to turn. Thank you. 

I called Wells Fargo on XXXX/XXXX/2015, approx. XXXX CST. 
The name I was given of the supervisor is XXXX XXXX.
